oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Capturing value with genomic testing
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Pratt, Paige
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: genomics
Health Heritability
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Utilizing genomic testing to select replacement heifers can in­crease the profitability of a cow herd. Veterinarians can assist producers in the selection of females that excel in multiple, and sometimes antagonistic, traits including stayability, heifer pregnancy, calving ease, growth and carcass traits. While an increase in pounds of calf produced or quality of carcass is easily calculated, other traits have been more elusive. It has been widely noted that the number one profit-driver for a com­mercial cow-calf operation is the longevity of the cow. When a cow has consecutive calvings, the breakeven of the calf price diminishes greatly over time. The health of cattle must also be considered when describing the profitability of a cow herd. Through genetic selection for improved heart health, we can find cattle with greater resilience to hypoxic events. Hypoxia can be caused by high altitude, respiratory disease, obesity or heat. The ability for that animal’s heart to be more resilient to the hypoxic event is moderately to highly heritable. Producers can utilize heart health genetic scores to help select seedstock and replacement females that will improve heart health leading to less morbidity and mortality from bovine congestive heart failure syndrome.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 6-7
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
